coredata.dc:title: Coupling of carbon dots in Eu3+ doped dicalcium silicate, derived from marine and agro-waste, offers a luminescent armor for counterfeiting, improving thermal sensing and advancing forensic explorations

coredata.dc:description: A series of red-emitting composite based on carbon dots in β-Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ (CDs@CSO:Eu3+) nanocomposites (NCs) were synthesized through a solid state (SS) method, aiming to enhance applications in latent fingerprints (LFPs), lip prints (LPs) detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) techniques and optical thermometry. The powdered X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis confirmed the monoclinic crystal structure of the phosphor. Under 394 nm excitation, the Eu3+ doped β-CSO:Eu3+ display a broad red emission peak at 615 nm, attributed to the 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+ ions. The optimal concentration of Eu3+ ions is determined to be 3 mol %, as higher concentrations led to a decrease in photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity due to concentration quenching (CQ). Additionally, a fabricated white light emitting diode (w-LED) using these phosphors achieved chromaticity coordinates of (0.355, 0.352) according to the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE), with the CIE, correlated colour temperature (CCT), and colour purity (CP) metrics indicating a bright green output with values of (0.6122, 0.3499), 1177 K, and 88.7%, respectively. The optimized 3wt % CDs@β-CSO:3Eu3+ composite demonstrated a remarkable CP of 97.7 %. Notably, the composite maintained 92.6 % of their emission intensity at 420 K, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is an impressive 85.8%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the process. This study explores the application of composite materials for LFPs detection and cheiloscopy. Utilizing advanced phosphor composites, we achieved enhanced visualization of fingerprints (FPs) features, including level I (ridge patterns), level II (minutiae points), and level III (ridge details such as pores and scars). Additionally, the composite's efficacy is demonstrated in cheiloscopy, capturing detailed LPs across type I to VI categories. The results highlight the composite's superior performance in both fingerprint and LP analysis, offering improved resolution and reliability for forensic applications. In addition, a novel approach is employed via brush mode to create AC patterns using optimized security ink. The resulting AC tags featured high resolution and durability. These findings underscore the 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:3Eu3+ composite as superior luminescent materials for use in fields requiring LFP, AC strategies, and optical thermometry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of red-emitting composite based on carbon dots in β-Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ (CDs@CSO:Eu3+) nanocomposites (NCs) were synthesized through a solid state (SS) method, aiming to enhance applications in latent fingerprints (LFPs), lip prints (LPs) detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) techniques and optical thermometry. The powdered X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis confirmed the monoclinic crystal structure of the phosphor. Under 394 nm excitation, the Eu3+ doped β-CSO:Eu3+ display a broad red emission peak at 615 nm, attributed to the 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+ ions. The optimal concentration of Eu3+ ions is determined to be 3 mol %, as higher concentrations led to a decrease in photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity due to concentration quenching (CQ). Additionally, a fabricated white light emitting diode (w-LED) using these phosphors achieved chromaticity coordinates of (0.355, 0.352) according to the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE), with the CIE, correlated colour temperature (CCT), and colour purity (CP) metrics indicating a bright green output with values of (0.6122, 0.3499), 1177 K, and 88.7%, respectively. The optimized 3wt % CDs@β-CSO:3Eu3+ composite demonstrated a remarkable CP of 97.7 %. Notably, the composite maintained 92.6 % of their emission intensity at 420 K, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is an impressive 85.8%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the process. This study explores the application of composite materials for LFPs detection and cheiloscopy. Utilizing advanced phosphor composites, we achieved enhanced visualization of fingerprints (FPs) features, including level I (ridge patterns), level II (minutiae points), and level III (ridge details such as pores and scars). Additionally, the composite's efficacy is demonstrated in cheiloscopy, capturing detailed LPs across type I to VI categories. The results highlight the composite's superior performance in both fingerprint and LP analysis, offering improved resolution and reliability for forensic applications. In addition, a novel approach is employed via brush mode to create AC patterns using optimized security ink. The resulting AC tags featured high resolution and durability. These findings underscore the 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:3Eu3+ composite as superior luminescent materials for use in fields requiring LFP, AC strategies, and optical thermometry.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been considerable attention in the luminescence domain towards rare-earth (RE) doped white light-emitting diodes. This interest stems from their capacity to generate precise, vibrant, and focused emission spectra. These diodes show potential across a multitude of applications, including solid-state lighting systems, optical sensors, displays, optoelectronic devices and lasers. Their increasing demand stems from their affordability, small size, low energy consumption, ease of maintenance, and high brightness. Currently, white light is produced by combining a blue-light-emitting GaN LED chip with YAG:Ce3+ yellow phosphor. However, this method has drawbacks, including a low colour rendering index (CRI) and a high correlated colour temperature (CCT) because it lacks a red-light component. Additionally, the production cost of these phosphors is notably high. Consequently, there is a considerable demand for achieving white light with desirable attributes, leading to an increased interest in using near-ultraviolet/UV chips along with green and red phosphors together with a blue LED chip. Currently, there is a trend of utilizing agricultural waste materials to create cost-effective, chemically and thermally stable, and efficient luminescent materials. Research suggests that agricultural and food waste materials can be effectively employed to create the necessary host materials with exceptional performance characteristics. Furthermore, this approach contributes to a decrease in environmental pollution and the creation of valuable products. The effectiveness of phosphor materials is influenced by both the host material and the dopants. Silicates stand out for their appeal as host materials because of their cost-efficiency, stable crystal structure, water resistance, and significant absorption in the near-UV spectrum. Calcium silicates, in particular, have demonstrated remarkable adaptability as luminescent hosts, thanks to their unique physical, chemical, and optical attributes. These features include strength at high temperatures, resistance to deformation over time, conductivity, chemical stability, low thermal expansion and thermal resilience. These calcium silicates can be produced using readily accessible agricultural and food waste materials rich in SiO2 and CaO content [1]

This report focuses on the utilization of paddy straw (PS) and cockle shells (CS) to produce calcium silicate, leveraging their status as prominent by-products of agro-food waste materials. Burning paddy straw generates new waste that poses environmental and health challenges, primarily due to its lightweight and small particle size. Disposing of ash diminishes the fertility of agricultural fields and contributes to air and water pollution. Paddy straw, composed primarily of 98 % SiO2 along with impurities, has restricted utility due to these impurities. On the other hand, pure SiO2, a crucial raw material, finds extensive use in ceramics, electronics, polymer industries, and various other sectors [2]. Cockle shells, originating from the coast of Southeast Asia (including Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia), are readily accessible as waste material. Cockle shells naturally contain CaCO3, a valuable substance with biomedical applications. Numerous reports in recent years have highlighted the use of CaCO3 NPs sourced from cockle shell waste in various areas such as drug delivery, polymer industries, biomedical scaffolds, and nanomedicine. Utilizing solid waste like cockle shells makes the process more environmentally friendly, sustainable, and economically viable [3]. Cockle shell waste, following heat treatment, contains approximately 92 % CaO. Initially, it consists of calcium carbonate (CaCO3), which, upon heat treatment, releases carbon dioxide (CO2) and converts into CaO. The combination of synthesized SiO2 and CaO materials has demonstrated an improvement in mechanical properties and a reduction in product costs. The enhanced thermal and mechanical characteristics of ceramics, attributed to their high melting points, demonstrate significant potential for industrial applications.

The Eu3+ ions exhibit symmetry characterized by a local inversion center. Its distinct pure red luminescence in the spectral region originates from the 4f shell, particularly the 4f6 configuration, which contains an even number of electrons. The crystal-field perturbation within each crystalline host disrupts either completely or partially the degeneracy of the 2S+1LJ levels. Among RE ions, the Eu3+ ion stands out as an extremely beneficial dopant due to its numerous transitions from the 5D0 excitation level to the 7FJ levels (J=0–4), making it suitable for mercury-free lamps and colour television displays. In line with this, the current study emphasizes the synthesis of phosphors using agricultural waste materials like paddy straw and cockle shells, serving as replacements for raw chemicals such as SiO2 and CaO, respectively. Eu3+ acts as a vibrant luminescent canter, showcasing appealing red luminescence through its 5D0→7FJ (J=0–4) transitions, resulting in exceptional red fluorescence. Traditional high-temperature solid-state reaction synthesis often leads to uneven particle shapes and sizes, despite the use of high temperatures and cumbersome grinding and sintering procedures. Additionally, there have been instances where this process resulted in the reduction of Eu3+ to Eu2+ valence states. As a result, various explanations have emerged in recent years aimed at reducing the calcination temperature and producing high-quality phosphor. Nevertheless, accomplishing straightforward and large-scale production of nanocrystals with a narrow particle size distribution and uniform morphology remains challenging. The solid-state reaction method is scientifically straightforward, adaptable, and versatile compared to alternative chemical routes. Considerable endeavours have been undertaken to enhance luminescent materials containing RE ions because of their potential applications in bio-imaging, display devices, medical fields, and beyond.

Sunitha et al. [4] recently investigated the luminescent properties of Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ phosphors for their potential application in w-LEDs. In their study, Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ displayed red emission at 613 nm with colour coordinates of (0.32, 0.34). In our current research, we have investigated the luminescent properties of Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ phosphors doped with different concentrations of Eu3+ (ranging from 1 to 5 mol %). The dominant transition observed is the 5D0→7F4 at 615 nm. Furthermore, in our study, the Eu3+ ions are found to occupy various sites within the CSO host material. It's noteworthy that our phosphors are synthesized using agricultural waste materials, specifically paddy straw ash as a source of SiO2 and cockle shells as a source of CaO, which differs from the materials used in Sunitha's work. The heightened intensity of the 5D0→7F4 transition compared to the magnetic dipole-allowed transition of 5D0→7F1 can be attributed to the principles of the 4f-4f intensity theory. This intensity difference is further explained by the influence of the local symmetry of the chemical environment surrounding the europium ions.

Among the array of calcium silicates, β-CSO stands out as a promising host material. Its attributes include a low phonon energy, wide bandgap, stable thermal, chemical and physical properties. β-CSO has garnered significant attention for its versatility across various applications. When doped with Eu3+ in β-CSO emits red light, making it a notable red light-emitting phosphor. While there is ample literature on the synthesis of β-CSO:Eu3+ phosphors, there is a lack of studies focusing on the structural, optical, and luminescent characteristics of these materials when produced using agricultural and food waste. This study aims to employ waste materials to create phosphors suitable for a range of solid-state lighting devices. The prospect of employing RE doped phosphors in solid-state lighting and colour displays has generated significant interest across all RE ions, with particular focus on the red-emitting trivalent Eu3+ ions. Table 1 [5-25] presents a comparison of β-CSO with various dopants.

Generally, nanomaterials doped with lanthanide ions often display diminished luminescence quantum efficiency, primarily as a result of factors like surface defects, low molar absorptivity, and narrow absorption bands. The luminescent characteristics of europium ions in β-CSO with a 2D/quasi 2D structure could be primarily influenced by surface defects, especially given the material's nanoscale thickness. Numerous successful strategies have been developed to boost the luminescence efficiency of nanomaterials doped with lanthanide ions. These methods encompass sensitizing GdVO4:Ln3+ with organic ligands, establishing core/shell structures to amplify luminescence, and utilizing plasmonic approaches to enhance up-conversion luminescence, among other techniques [26]. CDs are acknowledged as an innovative class of functional materials distinguished by exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, and other attributes. They offer potential for diverse applications such as imaging, biosensing, optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and beyond. Research has shown that incorporating CDs with other materials can greatly enhance the performance of the base materials. For instance, this combination has enhanced the Li ion storage capacity of TiO2 nanoribbons [27], improved the NO2 sensing capabilities of RGO [28], increased the photocatalytic activity of WS2 [29], strengthened the mechanical properties of hyperbranched epoxy [30], and enhanced the multifunctional characteristics of composite materials [31], among other benefits. There is limited literature available on utilizing CDs to improve the PL characteristics of nanomaterials doped with RE ions. Illustrative instances consist of NCs of Eu3+ doped LaF3@CDs and hierarchical structures of CDs@MgWO4:Eu3+. This research effectively produced Ca2SiO4:Eu3+ NPs utilizing a solid-state method. The study investigated the phase structure, morphology, and luminescence characteristics of these NPs, while also refining the synthesis conditions. Interestingly, the morphology and phase structure of the NPs are minimally affected by the type and concentration of Eu3+ ions employed for doping. Notably, the PL characteristics of the CSO:Eu3+ NPs are significantly enhanced by incorporating CDs to create CDs@ CSO:Eu3+ NCs. The PL emission intensities of CDs@CSO:Eu3+ NPs showed a twentyfold rise in comparison to the corresponding samples lacking CDs. The formation mechanism of CDs and CSO:Eu3+ NPs is extensively examined utilizing techniques such as PXRD, FTIR, XPS analysis, SEM, TEM, HR-TEM and SAED pattern. The study explored the mechanism driving the enhancement of luminescence concerning the energy levels of Eu3+ ions and CDs. This research not only introduces a synthesis approach for innovative 2D materials but also proposes a feasible strategy for improving the luminescence characteristics of nanomaterials doped with Ln3+ ions.

Europium (III) oxide (Eu2O3) with 99.99 % purity and urea (NH2CONH2) with 99.50 % purity are procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. These chemicals are all analytically pure and required no additional purification. Calcium oxide (CaO) and silicon dioxide (SiO2) are synthesized using bio-solid waste and agro-food waste, respectively.

CDs are synthesized using the hydrothermal method, where a solution containing 2.1 gm of citric acid and 0.009–0.094 gm of urea (0.1–1 wt %) dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water is prepared in a beaker. The solution is stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 5 min at room temperature before being transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel reactor with a Teflon liner for a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 14 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the solution is filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove larger particles. Subsequently, the solution undergoes dialysis using a 20 mL dialysis kit with a 1 kDa molecular weight cut-off, repeated eight times over 48 h in 1.5 L of distilled water. This process yields a pale-yellow solution of CDs. The synthesis mechanism of CDs utilizing urea and citric acid is depicted in Figure S1a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CaO from cockle shells
ce:section.ce:para: Cockle shells (CS) are rinsed with tap water to remove any attached egg white, then left to dry at room temperature for 12 h. Afterwards, the CS are crushed into small pieces. The crushed CS pieces are evenly distributed on a refractory rectangular tray with sufficient air circulation. The tray with CS is subsequently placed in a furnace set to 900 °C for 4 h for sintering. This procedure resulted in white powder, which is further finely ground to obtain CaO powder. A schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of CaO is depicted in Figure S1b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SiO2 from paddy straw
ce:section.ce:para: Paddy straw (PS) underwent a cleaning process with tap water to remove soil and dust. Following this, PS is allowed to air dry at room temperature for 24 h and then evenly distributed on an refractory rectangular tray with ample surface area. Flat trays are chosen to guarantee even dispersion of the paddy straw powder and adequate air circulation. The tray with PS is then placed in a furnace and sintered at 400 °C for 6 h and later at 800 °C for 8 h. This procedure yielded black paddy straw ash (PSA), which is finely ground and subjected to the alkaline digestion method using 1N NaOH. The alkaline digestion using NaOH resulted in a sodium silicate solution, which is subsequently treated with HCl for 18 h. Afterward, the solution underwent calcination to generate highly pure SiO2 (91%) derived from paddy straw. Figure S1c offers a schematic depiction of the SiO2 synthesis process.

Below is the chemical equation for the preparation of CSO:Eu3+ phosphors [32].
(1) 8CaO + 4 SiO2 + 2Eu2O3 → 4Ca2SiO4Eu + 3O2 ↑

According to the provided chemical equation, CaO, SiO2 and Eu2O3 is utilized in a stoichiometric proportion to produce the phosphors through the conventional high-temperature solid-state reaction method. CaO extracted from cockle shells, SiO2 obtained from paddy straw, and Eu2O3 (99.99 %) sourced from Sigma-Aldrich are measured in equimolar amounts, weighed using an electric balance, and subsequently ground in an agate mortar using acetone as the solvent. The finely ground powders are subjected to pre-sintering at 1250 °C for 8 h with intermittent grinding, followed by a subsequent final sintering at the same temperature for an additional 8 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the resulting phosphors are obtained. In Figure S1d, images of the prepared phosphors are displayed under ambient conditions and UV light. While no emission is detected under regular light, they emit a red hue under UV light. The intensity of the red hue augments with increased concentrations of Eu3+ ions.

Initially, 0.05 gm of the nanophosphor, dissolved in 3 mL of water, is added to CDs synthesized via hydrothermal methods. After agitation for 10 min, the mixture is transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and reacted for 8 h at 200 °C. The resultant sample is synthesized by washing the cooled solution with ethanol and subjecting it to three rounds of centrifugation at 4500 rpm. Given that the CDs are incorporated onto the CSO:3Eu3+ surface, dialysis is not necessary. The resulting sample is dried at 80 °C for 6 h prior to subsequent characterization and application (Figure S1e). Furthermore, the production of significant quantities of these NCs materials is uncomplicated and does not necessitate an extensive procedure.

White LED prototypes are fabricated through the integration of blue, green commercial phosphors, and CDs (3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs onto an InGaN chip with a peak wavelength of 394 nm. After being coated with a phosphor mixture, the LED devices are enclosed and cured at 150 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the packaged LED devices, now containing phosphor, underwent assessment within an integrating sphere under direct current forward-bias conditions.

The process initiated by dissolving powdered Poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) in 30 mL of DI water, followed by heating the mixture to 70 °C. Subsequently, CDs (3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, at the necessary concentrations, is dissolved in an 8 wt % PDMS solution. To ensure uniform dispersion, we utilize a probe sonicator for 30 min. The resulting AC ink, characterized by uniform dispersion, undergoes 15 min of degassing in a vacuum pumping system. Subsequently, the ink is cast and dried to produce transparent AC ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and visualization of Latent fingerprint and lip print
ce:section.ce:para: Ensure that the CDs (3 wt%)@CSO:3Eu³⁺ composite powder is thoroughly mixed to achieve uniform dispersion of the CDs within the CSO:3Eu³⁺ matrix. Obtain LFPs and LPs from 25 year old male donor and suggested to press fingers or lips gently against the desired substrate. Natural skin oils and sweat will leave an impression on the surface. Lightly dip a fine, soft brush into the CDs (3 wt%)@CSO:3Eu³⁺ composite powder. Gently tap off excess powder to avoid over-application. Carefully dust the powder onto the area where the latent print and lip print is suspected. Use gentle, sweeping motions to cover the surface evenly. Continue dusting until the latent fingerprint or lip print becomes visible. The powder should adhere to the residues left by the natural oils and sweat from the skin, revealing the pattern of the ridges or lip grooves. After the print is fully developed with the composite powder, place the substrate under a 365 nm UV lamp. The CDs (3 wt%)@CSO:3Eu³⁺ composite will emit bright red luminescence under UV light, enhancing the visibility of the fingerprint or lip print and capture using digital camera. Schematic representation of developmental procedure of LFPs and LLPs is depicted in Figure S2a & b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AC pattern development using luminescent security ink
ce:section.ce:para: For the creation of the high-visibility security ink, we combine PDMS (8% by weight) with CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. PDMS, renowned for its high viscosity (5000 cSt) and polymeric attributes, proves to be an excellent option for crafting enduring patterns on diverse substrates. The fluorescent labels are generated through various techniques, such as screen-printing, pen and simple brush mode across a variety of substrates. These fabricated FL can undergo visual inspection under UV 365 nm light. The process of creating luminescent patterns using the security ink in brush mode is depicted in Figure S2c.

Fig. 1 a shows the PXRD patterns of CaO and SiO2 samples. Upon calcination of cockle shell powder at 900 °C for 4 h, the emergence of a cubic phase CaO is observed which is consistent with JCPDS No. 82-1690 [33]. Furthermore, the PXRD analysis of SiO2 displayed a broad peak with maximum intensity at 2θ = 23.6 °, suggesting the amorphous nature of silica [34]. Fig. 1b displays the PXRD patterns of pure CSO and CSO:Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) samples, prepared without the presence of CDs. However, Fig. 1c shows shifting of diffraction peaks towards lower angle side. Fig. 1d exhibits the PXRD patterns following the blending of CDs with CSO:3Eu3+ NPs. It is evident that most of the peaks closely match the standard reference of CSO (ICDD card No. 01-083-0464) [22], and there are no noticeable changes observed in the PXRD patterns as the concentration of Eu3+ ions increase. This observation suggests that the doping of Eu3+ does not impact the CSO structure. The synthesized phosphors exhibited a Monoclinic structure, displaying a single phase with unit cell parameters of a = 5.5100 Å, b = 6.7600 Å, and c = 9.3200 Å. The angle β is measured at 94.50 °, resulting in a volume of 346.08 Å³. A specific peak at 2θ = 19.44 ⁰ is observed in the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, distinguishing them from samples without CDs. In Fig. 1d, (*) indicates peaks associated with CDs, while (♦) represents standard CSO peaks. The observed phenomenon could be attributed to the binding interactions between CDs and Ca2+ ions. It is widely acknowledged that the permissible percentage deviation in the ionic radii of dopant and host ions should not surpass 30 %. The radius percentage difference (Dr) between host ions (Ca2+) and doped ions (Eu3+) in CSO:Eu3+ is computed using the following formula [35]:
(2)
D r
=
[
R h
( CN )
−
R d
( CN )
]
R h
( CN )
×
100
%

In this context, CN: coordination numbers, Rh : the radius of the host cation, and Rd : the radius of the dopant. It is noteworthy for β-CSO that Ca2+ ions occupy two distinct non-equivalent sites. Ca(I) and Ca(II) are surrounded by 7 and 8 O atoms in their coordination spheres. The ionic radii for Ca(I) and Ca(II) sites are 1.06 Å and 1.12 Å, respectively. In contrast, the ionic radius of Eu3+ for 7 coordinated and 8-coordinated configurations is 1.11 Å and 1.06 Å, respectively. The permissible percentage deviation for 7 and 8 coordinated configurations in β-CSO:Eu3+ is determined to be 4.71 % and 4.82 %, respectively. This suggests that Eu3+ ions substitute Ca2+ ions at two distinct non-equivalent sites. Furthermore, the incorporation of Eu3+ ions into CSO does not induce significant alterations in the PXRD pattern. Detailed explanation and plots of crystallite size from various methods are discussed in supplementary section (Figure S3 & Table S1).

Further, an extensive structural Rietveld refinement of CSO:Eu3+ NPs at a suitable doping concentration using the FullProf software [36]. This is performed to verify the precise integration of Eu3+ ions into the CSO host matrix. Figures S4(a–f) exhibit the results of the Rietveld refinement of PXRD, which includes the calculated lattice parameters for CSO:Eu3+ NPs detailed in Table 2 . The structural refinement data for both pure CSO and CSO:Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) samples, along with the simulations, confirmed the monoclinic structure. The slight discrepancies in the statistical parameters (Rwp, Rp, χ2) imply the reliability of the Rietveld refinements. Analysis of the findings reveals that with the increase in Eu3+ content, the cell volume (V) consistently enlarges due to the effective substitution of Eu3+ cations into the CSO matrix [37]. The substitution of divalent Ca2+ cations with trivalent Eu3+ cations could potentially lead to a relaxation in the CSO host lattice. This occurs because, for every two Eu3+ cations replacing three Ca2+ cations, a Calcium vacancy (VCa) appears in the lattice, where Eu3+ Ca denotes the Eu3+ cations replacing Ca2+ sites [38].
(3) 3Ca2+ + 2Eu3+ → 2Eu3+ Ca + 1VCa

We utilized Diamond software [39] to generate the crystal structure of CSO:Eu3+, with Figure S4g displaying the resultant structure visually. Figure S4h illustrates polyhedral arrangement of CSO:Eu3+, while Figures S4(i–k) offer detailed information on inter-atomic distances, spacing between consecutive atoms, and bond angles, all of which are listed in Table S2. The Fourier electron density mapping is discussed in supplementary section (Figure S5)

Figure S6a depicts the FTIR spectra of SiO2, CaO, CSO, CDs and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs and corresponding mode assignments [40,41] are listed in Table S3. To gain further insight into how Eu doping influences the optical properties of CSO phosphors, UV-Vis spectra are recorded. Figure S6b depicts the reflectance spectra of both undoped and CSO: Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs, measured in the range 200–800 nm. The samples display an absorption band ranging from 200 to 250 nm, aligning with the charge transfer band of Eu3+→O2-. The variety of bands seen between 300–500 nm can be attributed to intra-configurational 4f-4f transitions that stem from the 7F0 ground-state level. The reduction in reflectance with doping is due to optical transitions taking place within the optical bandgap (Eg). The Kubelka-Munk (KM) function is a theoretical model used estimate the bandgap of phosphors. It transforms reflectance data into a function that is proportional to the absorption coefficient of the material. The KM function is particularly useful for estimating the bandgap of semiconductors from diffuse reflectance spectra. The basic form of the KM function, F(R), is given by:
(4)

( R )

)

Here, R represents the reflectance of the material. The Eg of the material and the KM function F(R) are interconnected through the following expression:
(5)

( R )

= C
(
h ν
− Eg
)
n

In this equation, where hν represents photon energy, C remains constant and n takes on values of ½ (direct allowed), 2 (indirect allowed), 3/2 (direct forbidden), and 3 (indirect forbidden), transitions. By utilizing Eq. (18), a plot relating to (F(R)hν)² vs hν (in electron volts) to determine the Eg, as illustrated in Figure S6c-h. Eg values are derived by extending the linear portion of the curves to the energy axis where (F(R)hν)² approaches zero. The resulting Eg values for both undoped and Eu-doped CSO are summarized in Table S4. Interestingly, it is evident that with an increase in the concentration of Eu3+ ions in the CSO host matrix, the Eg value decreases. While the change in the Eg may seem small, it has the potential to significantly impact the optical properties essential for various applications in optoelectronic devices. The reduction in Eg can be ascribed to several factors. One possible reason for this change might be linked to the degree of structural order-disorder within the lattice, which can alter the configuration of intermediate energy levels following the incorporation of Eu3+ ions.

In order to understand the luminescence performance of phosphors and NCs for solid-state lighting applications, surface morphology, particle size, and shape are important considerations. Using SEM and TEM, the samples micromorphology is examined to further illustrate the successful integration of CDs. On a semi-rigid surface, un-doped CSO displays small, regularly organized spherical formations (Fig. 2 a). Conversely, CSO:Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs has a solid surface with small spheres attached to it, resembling a bulk rock structure. The original phosphor morphology is not significantly altered by the incorporation of CDs, a feature that is difficult to observe directly in SEM images because of the small size of the CDs and the low mass loading. Fig. 2(b–f) unequivocally demonstrate that the produced compound is made up of agglomerated and irregular NPs.

Using EDX, the elemental makeup of every particle in the samples is examined. The typical peaks for the elements included into the lattice are evident during measurement because different portions of the chosen sample are focused on. Fig. 2(g–n) presents the results of the EDX-spectra analysis. Fig. 2g shows the presence of Ca, Si, O, and Eu elements in the analysis of the EDX spectra of CSO:3Eu3+, while Fig. 2h shows the same elements except C in the case of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+. In both CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ samples, oxygen is the most prevalent element, suggesting that there are no impurities or secondary elements present. The distribution of elements in both nano samples is dominated by oxygen, with calcium, silicon, and the doped element europium following in order of importance. Additionally, the elemental analysis of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ showed that, of all the atoms, carbon had the lowest weight percentage. The distribution of components like Ca, Si, O, Eu, and C is shown in Fig. 2(i–m). The inset of Fig. 2(g & h) displays the SEM micrographs together with the tables that display the weight and atomic percentages of each component element. Fig. 2n uses a bar graph to show the weight percentage of each element in the CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ samples.

Fig. 3 , which show TEM, HR-TEM, and selective area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, can help us better understand the growth mechanism with increasing concentrations of Eu3+ ions. A better knowledge of the morphologies of CDs, CSO:3Eu3+ NPs, and CDs(3 wt%)@CSO:Eu3+ NCs can be gained from these images. The produced CDs are uniformly spherical particles, according to TEM images (Fig. 3a), however the distribution of CSO:3Eu3+ is not uniform (Fig. 3b). TEM inspection reveals a markedly roughened surface on CDs(3 wt%)@CSO:3Eu3+, with uniformly dispersed small black spherical particles adhering uniformly to the CSO:3Eu3+ surface (Fig. 3c). This finding supports the successful recombination of CDs and CSO:3Eu3+ and further establishes the existence of CDs. The lattice fringes of CDs are easily visible in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 3d). While the lattice fringes of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ is shown in HR-TEM image of CDs (Fig. 3e). The inverse FFT pictures of CDs and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ are shown in Fig. 3(f, h & i) where the lattice fringes are clearly defined. The calculated distance between these fringes is 0.215 nm, which is equivalent to the (002) crystal plane of CDs [42]. Similarly, the distance between the lattice fringes corresponding to (200) and (002) planes of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ is found to be 0.269 nm and 0.215 nm respectively. The value for (200) plane is consistent with the monoclinic CSO (ICDD card No. 01-083-0464) lattice plane (200), providing a structural explanation for these observations. In addition, Fig. 3(g, k & l) presents pixel profiles showing interplanar spacing that are plotted using the GATAN digital micrograph software. Furthermore, well-scattered spots in an SAED pattern (Fig. 3j) provide more proof of the single crystalline features found in the samples that are acquired.

The chemical composition and valence state of the compounds different ions are examined using XPS technique and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 4 (a–l). Ca, Si, O, C, and Eu elements are present in the full scan XPS spectrum of the CSO:Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ (Fig. 4(a and g)). Consistent with the PXRD results, no further impurity elements are found. A Gaussian-Lorentzian function is used to fit the matching Ca 2p, Si 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and Eu 3d spectra. Different electronic spin states in CSO:3Eu3+ caused the Ca 2p peaks to separate into Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 components (Fig. 4b), with binding energies of 346.58 eV and 350.17 eV, respectively [43]. A conventional Si 2p3/2 and Si 2p1/2 spin-orbit doublet can be seen in the Si 2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4c) deconvoluted with binding energies of 102.66 eV and 103.34 eV, respectively. [44], whereas the binding energy is determined to be 530.87 eV for the O 1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 4d) [45]. The two characteristic bands in Fig. 4e, at 1133.10 and 1162.96 eV, correspond to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2, respectively, as can be seen in the high-resolution spectra of Eu 3d. Around 29.8 eV separates the binding energies of Eu 3d3/2 and Eu 3d5/2, indicating the presence of Eu3+ in the phosphor. A single peak at 284.52 eV [46] is visible in the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 4f) and is identified as the reference peak that surfaced during the study of CSO:3Eu3+ NPs. The Ca 2p high resolution spectra as typically separated into Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 with binding energies of 347.17 eV and 350.82 eV after CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs are examined [47]. While O 1s once more displays a single peak at 531.37 eV [48,49], which is attributed to the Si-O bond. Si 2p is once again deconvoluted into doublets, consisting of Si 2p3/2 at 103.37 eV and Si 2p1/2 at 103.82 eV (Fig. 4(h–j)). Similar to this, Fig. 4k shows the splitting of Eu 3d into Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2, with corresponding binding energies of 1133.79 and 1163.50 eV. Three peaks are identified in the deconvoluted high-resolution C 1s spectra at 284.69 eV, 286.02 eV, and 288.39 eV [46]. These peaks are identified as the sp3 bulk bonded carbon CC/CH, CO, and OCO bonds (Fig. 4l). According to this research, the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs surface possesses hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, indicating that the CDs and CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor have been successfully integrated. A comparative investigation of CSO:3Eu3+ NPs and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs is conducted using the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm is discussed in supplementary section (Figure S7).

The excitation spectrum of CSO:Eu3+ is illustrated in Fig. 5 a, covering a range from 200 nm to 500 nm. Significantly, when observed at 615 nm, the excitation spectrum exhibits two primary components. One component consists of a broad absorption band reaching its peak at 270 nm, whereas the other comprises a sequence of narrow-band peaks ranging from 350 nm to 500 nm. The distinct excitation peaks correlate with the f-f transition of the Eu3+ ion, with one particularly notable peak occurring at 394 nm (7F0→5L6). This wavelength, 394 nm, serves as the optimal excitation wavelength and is notable for its compatibility with InGaN chips [50,51]. Consequently, CSO:Eu3+ phosphors are well-suited for packaging in w-LEDs. In Fig. 5b, the emission spectrum of CSO:3Eu3+ under 394 nm excitation is depicted. The phosphor demonstrates multiple emission peaks at 579 nm, 591 nm, 615 nm, 654 nm, and 702 nm, aligning with the Eu3+ ion transitions 5D0→7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) respectively. Notably, the 5D0→7F2 transition at 615 nm is associated with the distinct red emission. In the case of Eu3+ ion transitions, Magnetic Dipole (MD) luminescence (5D0→7F1) is dominant when Eu3+ ions are in a symmetric position, whereas electric dipole (ED) luminescence (5D0→7F2) prevails when Eu3+ ions are in an asymmetric position [52]. The Electric Dipole (ED) transition is notably more prominent than the MD transition, with an IED/IMD ratio of 5.780. This finding implies that the Eu3+ ion has assumed the asymmetric position within the CSO structure. Furthermore, in the CSO crystal, the Ca2+ ion demonstrates an asymmetric distribution in the lattice. Taking these factors into account, it can be deduced that the substitution of Ca2+ ions by Eu3+ ions contributes to the intense red light emission observed. Fig. 5c illustrates the emission spectra of CSO:Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs under 394 nm excitation. These phosphors demonstrate comparable emission spectra, showing no noticeable wavelength shifts or peak deviations at higher Eu3+ doping concentrations. Remarkably, with the increase in doping concentration, the emission intensity escalates, reaching its peak at a doping concentration of 3 mol %. However, beyond this threshold (x = 3 mol %), the emission intensity does not exhibit further augmentation but rather experiences a slight decline, attributed to the concentration-quenching effect (Inset of Fig. 5c). Hence, the optimal doping concentration for the CSO host is determined to be 3 mol %, surpassing that of Na2Ca1-xP2O7:xEu3+ (x=0.05 mol %) [53], La2ZnTiO6:xEu3+ (x=0.08 mol %) [54], and Na3Y(VO4)2:Eu3+ (x=0.15 mol %) [55], but falling short of certain stoichiometric phosphors like LiEu(PO3)4 [56], Ca3Eu2B4O12 [57], and K3Eu(PO4)2 [58]. These stoichiometric phosphors exhibit a beneficial non-concentration-quenching trait, enabling high doping concentrations without compromising luminescence intensity. This attribute makes them suitable for solid-state lighting applications. Moreover, the significant Eu3+ doping concentration suggests the potential for strong luminescence in the phosphor. To investigate the interaction mechanism between Eu3+ ions at high doping concentrations, the critical distance (Rc) is computed using Blasse's Equation [59]:

Where, Rc denotes the critical distance, V represents the volume of the unit cell, Xc indicate the optical doping concentration of CSO and N is the chemical units of the unit cell. For CSO:Eu3+ phosphors, with specific values for V (346.08 Å3), Xc (3), and N (2), the resulting Rc is determined to be 7.02 Å, exceeding the 5 Å threshold [60]. The calculation data unveils the CQ mechanism, which is ascribed to multipole-multipole interactions. Furthermore, the precise interaction mechanism among Eu3+ ions in this concentration-quenching phenomenon can be elucidated using the following Eq. (7), derived from Dexter's theory [61]:

Fig. 5d illustrates the plot of a linear correlation between log(X) and log(I/X). The slope, denoted as −α/3, is approximately −0.85, indicating α to be around 2.5, which closely resembles the value of 3. This value of α indicates different types of multipole-multipole interactions: α = 3 (nearest neighbour ions interaction), α = 6 (dipole-dipole interaction), α = 8 (dipole-quadrupole interaction), and α = 10 (quadrupole-quadrupole interaction). Thus, the concentration-quenching observed in CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor can be attributed to nearest neighbour ions interaction.

The nanophosphor is evaluated for its colour emission capability and purity using the CIE-1931 system [63]. In Fig. 5e, the CIE colour diagram of the CSO:Eu3+(1–5 mol %) nanophosphor is illustrated. From the CIE diagram, it is noted that the sample containing 3 mol % Eu3+ exhibits a vivid red hue (0.6122, 0.3499). These coordinates are compared with the standard coordinates of the National Television Standards Committee (NTSC) for red emission (0.63, 0.34) and are found to closely match. These results demonstrate remarkable red-emission performance in the manufactured samples, indicating promising potential for LED manufacturing. Additionally, the correlated colour temperature (CCT) value is calculated for the prepared samples, as shown in Fig. 5f. The McCamy's empirical formula, provided below, is utilized to determine the average CCT value [64]:

The colour epicentre (xe = 0.333, ye = 0.184) and the CIE coordinates (x, y) are utilized to calculate the CCT, which is tabulated in Table 3 . According to this calculation, the highest red emission is observed at approximately 1177 K, highlighting the potential of the NPs in LED device fabrication. The CP is calculated using the coordinates (xs, ys) for different dopant concentrations, the dominant wavelength coordinates (xd, yd) set at (0.685, 0.327), and the illuminating point coordinates (xi, yi) at (0.33, 0.33). Among these, CSO:3Eu3+ exhibited the highest CP value of 88.7%.

In recent times, novel approaches have surfaced to enhance the luminescence of phosphors. One such method involves the cultivation of a textured layer of crystalline anisotropic SrAl2O4:Eu, Dy on sapphire, which resulted in a doubling of its phosphorescence efficiency. Moreover, the luminous efficiency of phosphors has been effectively enhanced by incorporating CDs [66-68]. Examining the PLE and PL spectra of CDs (Fig. 6 a) offers insights into their energy absorption and emission traits within CDs@CSO:Eu3+(1–4 wt %). The PL spectrum of CDs unveiled a broad peak at 405 nm [69], closely matching the PLE peak of Eu3+ at 394 nm (Fig. 6b). The detected PLE peak in CDs is linked to electron transitions occurring between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO, π) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO, π*) energy levels within CDs [70–72]. The blue emission observed in CDs is influenced by the π⁎→π transition occurring within the core states. Yet, the presence of surface states led to a narrowing of energy levels, facilitating broad blue-green emission as a result of electron transitions from corresponding π⁎ →π orbitals. The mix of emissions from core and surface states produced an asymmetrical PL spectral pattern, with a PL peak detected at 405 nm for CDs. The broad PL spectra provide a notable benefit by facilitating a considerable spectral overlap between the PL of CDs and the PLE of phosphors, ensuring efficient energy transfer [73,74], as illustrated in Fig. 6b. CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ displayed increased spectral overlap, enhancing the efficiency of excitation energy transfer from CDs to CSO:Eu3+ NPs. The absorption of excitation energy (λexci = 338 nm) by CDs effectively took place, succeeded by its transmission to Eu3+ through either the Förster or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, ultimately resulting in radiative emission. As a result, the PL intensity of CDs (3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs is enhanced by 4 folds compared to that of CSO:3Eu3+ (Fig. 6c), but then experienced intensity quenching. The PL intensity of CDs@CSO:Eu3+ NCs is notably higher than that of CSO:Eu3+. This increase can be attributed to the FRET between CDs and Eu3+ ions. The FRET phenomenon hinges on three key criteria in theory: Firstly, the donor and acceptor molecules must be in close proximity (1–10 nm). This proximity is evidenced by TEM image (Fig. 6c) of the CDs@CSO:Eu3+ nanocomposite, illustrating the strong adherence of CDs to the CSO:Eu3+ surface. Secondly, the acceptor's absorbance spectrum must overlap with the donor's luminescence emission spectrum, facilitating energy transfer in the spectral crossover region shown in Fig. 6b. Lastly, the alignment of donor and acceptor transition dipole orientations is typically parallel, enabling energy transfer through dipole-dipole interactions.

Upon excitation at 394 nm, electrons in the CDs are raised to the excited state, leading to two possible outcomes. They may either non-radiatively transfer energy to the acceptor (Eu3+) or radiatively relax through fluorescence. The mechanism underlying luminescence enhancement involves the transition of electrons in Eu3+ from the 7F0 state to various components of the excited 5D energy level, while electrons in CDs transition from HOMO to LUMO states with absorbed photon energy, as depicted in Fig. 6d. Subsequently, the excited electrons relax to core and surface state energies, transitioning to the 5D0–2 energy levels of Eu3+ through the FRET process [75,76].

Fig. 6(e & f) presents the (CIE 1931) colour chromaticity and CCT diagrams of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. Notably, as the concentration of CDs increases, the emission intensity rises but then decreases after reaching 3 wt %. The 3 wt % CDs@3Eu3+ sample displays an intense red colour (0.6699, 0.3222). Calculated CCT values confirm that the highest red emission is observed at around 1000 K. The obtained CCT values are below 3000 K, indicating the NCs suitability for white LED applications. The maximum CP reached 97.7% using Eq. (24). Details of the CIE coordinates, calculated CCT, and CP are provided in Table 3. As shown in Fig. 6(e & f), the CIE coordinates of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ show minor changes from 1 to 4 wt %, implying a very slight variation in the color of the phosphor and the NCs. These findings suggest that the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs have potential applications in LFP, LLP, AC, and lighting devices.

The assessment of optical stability plays a pivotal role in evaluating the practical applicability of luminescent materials. Therefore, the fluorescence and thermal stability of CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ are investigated to determine their capability to sustain PL intensity, as illustrated in Fig. 6(g–i). Following 50 days of exposure to ambient conditions and 300 min of continuous UV-light exposure, CSO:3Eu3+ retains 95 % and 98 % of its initial PL strength, respectively. Similarly, the emission stability of CDs(3 wt%)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs remains at 96 % and 99 % of the original intensity after the same treatment (Fig. 6g & h). Furthermore, both CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ exhibit consistent luminescent properties even after heating at 480 K for 6 h (Fig. 6i). Adding CDs to the phosphor effectively reduces the degradation and clumping of the luminescent center when exposed to heat.

JO (Judd-Ofelt) theory is highly valuable for predicting various optical properties such as oscillator strengths, luminescence branching ratios, radiative lifetimes of excited states, and quantum efficiencies, all based on just three parameters derived from absorption or luminescence spectra. This theory has become fundamental in RE optical spectroscopy. However, its complexity makes manual calculations laborious, and commercial software often has limitations. To address these challenges, JOES software has been developed, offering a more efficient and comprehensive solution [77].

The uniqueness of europium within the RE elements is notable as JO analysis can be conducted using both emission and absorption spectra. Europium's distinctiveness stems from its Magnetic dipole (MD) transition, specifically the 5D0→7F1 transition, whose dipole strength remains constant regardless of the environment. This stability allows for precise calculations and serves as a reliable reference for transitions originating from 5D0 [78,79] (1esu = N−5/2 cm).

The JO theory is employed to delve into the site symmetry and luminescent characteristics of Eu3+ ions embedded within the CSO host matrix. Analysing the transition intensity parameters referred to as JO parameters Ωt (where t = 2, 4, 6) offers valuable insights into the structural alterations and bonding environment surrounding the Eu3+ ions embedded within the host matrix. The estimation of these parameters typically relies on the emission spectra, which are influenced by the distinct energy levels of Eu3+ ions. Specifically, calculations for Ω2 and Ω4 are associated with the 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively. However, the determination of the intensity parameter Ω6, corresponding to the 5D0→7F6 transition, is not feasible due to instrumental constraints that precluded the observation of this transition. The computation of these intensity parameters follows the methodology outlined by Kodaira et al. The JO parameters (Ω2, Ω4) and additional spectral characteristics obtained through JOES software are detailed in Table 4 . A noticeable difference is observed between the values of Ω2 and Ω4, signifying the prominence of the 5D0→7F2 transition over the 5D0→7F4 transition. Ω2 is notably sensitive to changes in the ligand environment, evident from its fluctuations with varying concentrations of Eu3+ ions in the host lattice. Branching ratio values are notably higher for the 5D0→7F2 transition compared to others, underscoring its significance. Additionally, with a branching ratio exceeding 50%, this transition stands out as the primary one, highlighting the efficacy of the synthesized phosphors for lighting purposes [83].

Thermal stability is a crucial aspect assessed for WLED applications operating at elevated temperatures (> 400 K). The PL intensity shows a slight decline as the temperature rises for both CSO:3Eu3+ (Fig. 7 (a–b)) and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ (Fig. 7(c–d)). At 420 K, the integrated intensity of Eu3+ emission reaches 85.0% for CSO:Eu3+ and 92.6% for CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 7e), demonstrating significantly better thermal stability in the CD-incorporated NCs compared to the phosphor alone. Comparisons of residual intensity at 420 K with other phosphors are tabulated in Table 5 [84-89]. The activation energy (Ea) for thermal quenching is calculated using the Arrhenius Equation, as referenced in [90].

The Ea values are 0.27 eV for CSO:3Eu3+ and 0.321 eV for CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, as illustrated in Fig. 7(f & g). The comparison of Ea for various phosphors along with present material is presented in Table 6 [91-96].

Fig. 7h illustrates the configuration coordinates of Eu3+ ions. Here, the electron undergoes excitation from ground state to the highest energy level 5L6 upon stimulation with a 394 nm wavelength (process a), followed by entry into the 5D0 level via non-radiative transition (NR, process b). At room temperature, the majority of electrons revert to the ground state (7FJ) through luminescence. However, as temperatures increase, more electrons surpass the energy barrier (Ea) and reach the CTB (process c) without reverting to the ground state. These electrons are then discharged from the CTB to the ground state through another non-radiative transition (process f) known as phonon relaxation. As the temperature increases, the interaction between electrons and phonons intensifies, leading to more electrons relaxing non-radiatively to the ground state. Typically, a higher Ea value signifies more challenging thermal quenching, indicating better thermal stability for CSO:Eu3+ phosphors. Electrons trapped in the system will reach the 5D0 energy level through thermal activation (processes d and e) at an appropriate temperature, enhancing emission intensity as they return to the ground state. With a higher temperature, the majority of trapped electrons revert to the 5D0 state, mitigating the abnormal thermal quenching phenomenon and reducing energy consumption [97]. Consequently, the CSO:Eu3+ phosphors exhibit outstanding thermal stability, making them suitable for developing LFPs in challenging environments.

The CIE coordinates serve as crucial indicators for assessing the efficacy of a phosphor or NCs. Fig. 7i illustrates the CIE diagram of CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs across temperatures ranging from 300 K to 480 K. The chromaticity coordinates clustered near the red zone signify their high CP. Table S5 presents the CIE coordinates of the NCs at different temperatures. The data clearly show a decreasing trend in CIE x with rising temperature, while CIE y exhibits an increasing trend. The shift in CIE coordinates, denoted as Δx and Δy (Δx = 0.02153 and Δy = 0.02108) (Fig. 7j), indicates excellent thermal stability of this NCs. Additionally, employing Eq. (22) allowed for the calculation of CCT values for the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, as presented in Table S5. CP, determined using Eq. (24), is also assessed to evaluate the NCs performance. Consequently, the CP of the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ (300–480 K) phosphor ranged from 97.2 % to 97.7 %. These results suggest that the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs could be a viable choice of fluorescent materials for white LEDs.

Based on the equations mentioned above and the fitted results shown in the insets of Fig. 8(a & b), the value of ∆E can be derived from the FIR-temperature relationship. Subsequently, the obtained SA and SR, which depend on temperature, are plotted in Fig. 8(c & d). It is evident that SA is influenced by the FIR values. The SA and SR exhibit an approximately parabolic variation with temperatures, and the SR reaches a maximum of 3.09 and 3.48 % K−1 at 300 K, respectively, for CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+. Remarkably, the temperature SR studied for CSO:Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ surpasses that of the compared materials, as illustrated in Table 7 [99-104]. Furthermore, the repeatability and stability of these two optical temperature sensing materials are examined, as depicted in Fig. 8(e & f). The remarkable relative sensitivity and stability imply that both the synthesized CSO:Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ could be regarded as promising candidates for accurate non-contact optical temperature sensing applications.

IQE measures the proportion of photons emitted as secondary fluorescence to the number of primary emission photons produced from absorbed excitation light within a defined time interval, typically measured in seconds. It assesses the luminous efficacy of fluorescent materials and can be computed using the following formula [105]:

The equation illustrates the quantum efficiency, where ER represents the emission spectrum of BaSO4, ES denotes the integrated intensity of the phosphor and LS signifies the emission spectrum of the phosphor. The IQE graph for CSO:Eu3+ phosphor and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs is shown in Fig. 8g & h respectively. The calculated IQE values are 77.6 % for the phosphor and 85.8 % for the NCs. The IQE is determined to be remarkably high at 85.8 % for the CDs incorporated CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, showcasing exceptional luminescence characteristics. Since IQE is a crucial metric for assessing phosphors application performance, we compared the prepared phosphor and NCs with several reported Eu3+ doped phosphors Table 8 [106-113]. As depicted in Fig. 8i, the CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs from this study exhibited a higher IQE . The comparison of IQE with different phosphors is detailed in Table 8. These findings highlight the significant potential of the NCs in w-LED applications.

The decay lifetimes for CDs (λexc = 338 nm and λemi = 405 nm), CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor (λexc = 394 nm and λemi = 615 nm) and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs (λexc= 394 nm and λemi = 615 nm) are determined, and their respective lifetime values are presented in Fig. 9 (a–c). In this context, ‘I’ denotes the emission intensity at time ‘t’, characterized by constants A1 and A2 , with τ1 and τ2 representing the double exponential components of decay time. The lifetime of CDs is approximately 11.8 ns. Double exponential decay is frequently observed in luminescent materials because of energy transfer mechanisms. The decay profiles of CSO:3Eu3+ NPs and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs are more accurately represented by a double exponential function rather than a single exponential function, as depicted in Fig. 9(b & c). Such dual exponential decay patterns are frequently encountered in luminescent materials experiencing energy transfer processes. Utilizing the provided equations, the mean lifetimes of CSO:3Eu3+ and CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs are found to be 2.68 ms and 3.85 ms, respectively. The extended decay time observed in the composite, in contrast to the plain phosphor, may lead to donor-acceptor pair recombination, contributing to luminescence. Adding CDs to CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor enhances the composites surface area. As the surface area grows with reduced particle size, more surface defects emerge, potentially serving as luminescent centers [116]. The variation in the lifetimes of CDs and the CDs incorporated nanocomposite is due to energy transfer between CDs and the CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor which is well described by the FRET mechanism.

Prototype w-LED devices are developed using a 395 nm InGaN chip along with a commercial BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+ blue phosphor, (Sr, Ba)2SiO4:Eu2+ green phosphor [87] and the prepared CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ red NCs, with a weight ratio of blue, green, and red phosphor set at approximately 2:1:5. The electroluminescence (EL) spectra of these w-LED devices align well with their corresponding PL spectra, indicating stable luminescence properties (Fig. 9d). Moreover, the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the resulting warm white LED device, under a 20 mA current excitation, are located at (0.355, 0.352) (Fig. 9e) highlighting the practical application potential of CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs for creating healthy and warm w-LEDs.

The CSO:3Eu3+ phosphor and the CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs material are employed as fluorescent labelling agents for detecting LFPs via the traditional powder dusting technique. Before preparing the FPs, the participants diligently washed their fingers with soap and water, then dried them gently by blowing air. Subsequently, they gently pressed their fingers against oily areas of their nose, forehead, or hair, transferring the prints onto various substrate surfaces with the appropriate pressure to gather latent fingerprint samples. Following this, the samples are left to air-dry for 1–2 min. Then, the phosphor is sprayed onto the substrate, and any surplus powder is carefully eliminated using a rubber suction ball. Lastly, the fingerprint is photographed using a Nikon D7500 DSLR Camera with an 18–140 mm lens, illuminated by UV 365 nm light. Examining the contrast of fingerprint displays aids in assessing the capability of the phosphor and NCs for fingerprint visualization. In Fig. 10(a & d), fluorescence images of female and male FPs dusted with CSO:3Eu3+, CDs(x)@CSO:3Eu3+ (x = 1, 2, 3, and 4 wt %) powders respectively, are presented, allowing for a visual analysis of the intensified red emission observed in the FPs dusted with CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. The structural attributes of FPs dusted with CDs@CSO:Eu3+ NCs consist of staggered ridges and grooves. These FPs exhibit distinctive characteristics across three levels: Level I (typical features), Level II (visible features), and Level III (micro features). Fig. 10 (b & c) depicts the overall and specific characteristics of male and female FPs left on aluminum foil surfaces. Core (Loop/Whorl/Arch) and delta are both part of Level I features. These Level I features can be utilized for a rough classification of FPs, but they do not provide sufficient information to determine the individual's identity. Hence, it becomes crucial to examine the Level II fingerprint features. Enclosure, ridge end, bridge, bifurcation, eye, dot, and incipient ridges are all part of Level II features. These Level II characteristics enable the distinction of individual identity traits as the combination of these visible details is distinctive to each person. Equally important to Level II, Level III also plays a crucial role in distinguishing individual identity traits. Sweat pores, creases, scars, ridge width, and ridge shapes are among the characteristics of Level III features. Sweat pores are pivotal in identifying distinctive traits of individuals, particularly in situations where FPs are incomplete or damaged [117]. Typically, sweat pore diameters range from 88 to 220 µm [118].

It is essential to investigate the ability of phosphors to visualize LFPs on various substrates. We assessed the sensitivity of our NCs by examining FPs on different surfaces (Fig. 11 (a–f)): leaf, paper, glass, stainless steel, wood, and plastic. Substrates can be categorized into three classes: porous, semi-porous, and non-porous. Porous substrates encompass leaf and paper, non-porous substrates comprise glass and stainless steel, whereas, semi-porous substrates consist of wood and plastic. Clearly, the fingerprint features of three levels can be discerned on various substrates, as illustrated in Fig. 11(a–f). The pixel profiles enabled the distinct identification of ridge and furrow patterns. Additionally, the pixel profiles are generated using ImageJ software of chosen area of FPs (Fig. 11(a1-f1)) on diverse substrates provided the average distance between consecutive ridges. 3D interactive plots corresponding to each substrate are presented in Fig. 11(a11-f11). This confirms the significant potential of the CDs(3 wt %) @CSO:3Eu3+ NCs in latent fingerprint analysis.

Figure S8(a–d) illustrates the effect of ageing, temperature exposure, UV exposure, and solvent treatment on LFPs dusted with our NCs powder. Remarkably, after 240 days, the FPs maintain nearly all of their features (Figure S8(a)). The stability of the developed FPs on the overhead projector (OHP) sheet under various temperature conditions (25 °C, 50 °C, 75 °C, 100 °C, 150 °C, and 200 °C) is investigated and is depicted in Figure S8(b). As depicted in the figure, the luminescent properties of the produced FPs can endure temperatures of up to 200 °C. This suggests that the synthesized NCs is an excellent option for LFPs visualization applications across a broad spectrum of environmental conditions. In Figure S8(c), there is minimal visible alteration in the latent fingerprint (LFP) images, suggesting the exceptional luminous stability of the CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs when exposed to 365nm UV light. Subsequently, we examined the response of the prepared NCs to various solvents (Figure S8(d)). Even after subjecting the CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ dusted LFPs to various vapours, they preserved all their characteristics without interacting with the respective solvents. The corresponding gray value plots (Figure S8(a1-d1)) show only minor fluctuations, retaining a significant distinction between their peaks and valleys. This indicates that LFP images can remain distinctly visible when illuminated using the CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, even after prolonged preservation, exposure to high temperatures, treatment with various solvents, and extended UV light exposure. Given these findings, the CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs could be more efficiently employed in the realm of forensic LFPs identification.

Sweat pores represent a significant characteristic of LFPs developed on glass substrate (Fig. 12), exhibiting diverse shapes such as triangular, circular, oval, and square. These distinct shapes offer valuable insights for the identification and comparison of FPs. The shape of sweat pores can be affected by a range of factors, encompassing genetics and environmental influences. Through the examination of sweat pore shapes within a latent fingerprint, forensic specialists can extract valuable information regarding the identity of the individual who deposited the print. In the comprehensive examination for personal identification, numerous characteristics are taken into account. These include factors such as the (i) pore count, (ii) inter-spacing between pores, (iii) sweat pore area, (iv) pore shape (whether circular, square, or triangular), and (v) sweat pore positioning (whether central, open at one end, or open at both ends). These aspects collectively contribute to the detailed analysis and comparison of FPs for accurate identification purposes. The average number of pores per unit length stands as a crucial measure for quantifying pores. Fig. 12b demonstrates the pore count observed in images obtained through the powder dusting technique with CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. In this context, the number of pores along the length of friction ridges can vary significantly, typically falling within the range of 11 to 25 pores per unit length. Previous research conducted by Locard, O'Leary et al., and Bindra et al. has provided insights into these variations [119]. They reported ranges of 9 to 20 friction ridges/cm of friction ridge and 8 to 25 pores/cm of epidermal ridge, respectively. Additionally, sweat pores exhibit diversity in their positions, with some being centrally located and others appearing open at one or both ends. These characteristics contribute to the unique and intricate nature of FPs, enabling their use in forensic analysis and personal identification. The adherence of NCs to the edge of pores plays a crucial role in determining whether a sweat pore appears open or closed within a fingerprint. This phenomenon highlights the importance of the interaction between the NCs material and the pore edge. The examination reveals the existence of circular, oval, square, and triangular pores.

The interspacing distance of consecutive sweat pores and the areas they cover are presented in Fig. 12(c &e). The estimated pores sizes are in the range 89–184 μm, as illustrated in Fig. 12d. Various factors come into play, including the pressure applied during the deposition of FPs, the consistency of the NCs material used, and the specific technique employed for print development. These factors can collectively influence the appearance and characteristics of pores within the fingerprint, leading to variations in pore attributes observed during analysis. Understanding and considering these factors are essential for accurate interpretation and evaluation of fingerprint evidence in forensic investigations. As a result, the likelihood of two pores extracted from the same finger of the same individual being identical is exceedingly low. This assertion underscores the unique and intricate nature of FPs, where even the smallest features, such as pores, exhibit variability. Oklevski [120] highlights that pores present diverse shapes such as circles, triangles, squares, and others, varying in size. However, it's important to note that pore shapes are not fixed, as distortions due to pressure can modify their fundamental forms. The pore shapes evident in the developed LFPs are shown in Fig. 12f. Contrary to earlier assertions in studies [121,122] that circular pores are predominant, recent research indicates a prevalence of circular, triangular, and square pores, with rhomboid and rectangular pores being less frequently encountered. Fig. 12g illustrates pores positioned along the ridges of the fingerprint, which are developed on aluminum foil. Additionally, it provides a visual representation of sweat pores observed in aged fingerprint images. These observations emphasize the complexity and distinctiveness of individual FPs, further reinforcing their utility in forensic analysis and personal identification. Moreover, their respective 3D surface plots are depicted in Fig. 12(g1-i, ii, iii). The illustrations underscore that closed pores, whether singular or on both sides, are more commonly observed than open pores.

LP serves as another significant identifying feature for distinguishing between individuals. Fig. 13 (a & b) displays latent LPs of male and female donors, respectively, dusted with CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs powder and photographed under 365 nm UV light. LPs are classified into six regions: upper left (UL), upper middle (UM), upper right (UR), lower left (LL), lower middle (LM), and lower right (LR). In contrast to FPs, LPs do not display three levels of features. Instead, they possess a single level of features, which encompass various patterns including complete vertical groove (Type I), partial vertical groove (Type I1), branched groove (Type II), intersected pattern (Type III), reticular pattern (Type IV), and undifferentiated patterns (Type V) [123,124]. Fig. 13(a & b) illustrates the various types of lip print characteristics observed in female and male LPs, respectively. Quadrant wise statistical analysis of female and male LPs are shown in Fig. 13(a1 & b1). Statistical analysis of overall LPs of male and female reveals that in female lip print, the order of prevalence is Type III > Type I1 > Type II > Type I > Type V > Type IV, while in male lip print, it is Type Iꞌ > Type I > Type II > Type V > Type III > Type IV (as shown in Fig. 13(a11 & b11)). Six characteristics and their respective regions can effectively differentiate between individuals, a factor of great importance in the field of forensic science. Furthermore, LPs from four distinct donors are captured on an overhead projector (OHP) sheet, and the pixel profiles and 3D interactive plots of these prints can be seen in Figure S9. The utilization of CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ demonstrates significant potential and sensitivity in visualizing LPs, as evidenced by their observation on various surfaces including porous (paper, marble), semi-porous (wood, plastic), and non-porous (stainless steel plate, glass) as depicted in Figure S9(a–f). Additionally, Figure S9(a1-f1 and a11-f11) presents the corresponding grey value plots and 3D interactive plots of the respective LPs.

LPs developed using fluorescent phosphors are valuable in forensic science for their ability to enhance the visibility of unique lip patterns under specific lighting conditions, such as UV light. The stability of these fluorescent phosphors over time is crucial for maintaining the clarity and readability of the developed lip prints. Figure S10a-d shows the aged LPs up to 360 days. In the initial period (up to 360 days), the fluorescent optimized composite retains its luminescent properties effectively. During this time, the composite particles remain intact, and their ability to emit light under UV excitation is stable. This stability can be attributed to the high-quality synthesis of the phosphor materials, proper application techniques, and optimal environmental storage conditions that prevent premature degradation. Further, interactive plots of LPs provide a powerful tool for forensic analysis, offering a detailed, multi-dimensional view of LPs features that significantly enhance visualization and examination. These plots are created using ImageJ software to capture the intricate details of LP groove patterns. Figure S10a1-d1 shows the interactive plots of aged LPs developed on glass surface. The results clearly indicate that forensic analysts more accurately identify and classify these critical features (type I-VI), which are key for LP identification. Additionally, Pixel profiling of LFPs provides a detailed analysis of the pixel intensity variations across a LP image. This technique is essential for enhancing LP visualization, understanding the quality of the print, and improving the accuracy of LP identification. As can be seen from the Figure S10a11-d11, a well-defined profile with distinct groove patterns indicates clear ridge and valley patterns.

The CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs fabricated in this study introduce exciting possibilities for practical AC (alternating current) applications due to their outstanding photoluminescent (PL) properties. These NCs exhibit unique characteristics that make them highly suitable for various applications in AC systems. Their remarkable PL properties, stemming from the combined effects of CDs and Eu3+ ions, enhance their potential applications in optoelectronic devices. Additionally, their development opens avenues for further exploration and utilization of advanced materials in AC technologies, contributing to the advancement of energy-efficient and high-performance systems. Counterfeiting and falsification of essential goods and documents, such as currency, certificates, pharmaceuticals, well-known brands and more, pose significant global challenges impacting humanity, the economy, and social advancement. Despite the utilization of diverse fluorescent materials in AC applications, several challenges persist, including issues related to toxicity, spectral overlap, low quantum efficiency, and luminescence quenching [125-127]. To showcase the potential utility of the synthesized NCs for AC purposes, luminescent-based security inks are developed. To contrast the CSO:3Eu3+ NPs and CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs, two images of the same pattern are created on craft paper via brush mode, and the differences can be observed visually in Fig. 14 (a & b). The image drawn using CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs fluorescent ink exhibits intense luminescence, allowing for clear visibility of each detail compared to the image drawn using security ink prepared with CSO:3Eu3+ NPs. Additional AC designs are generated using brush mode on various surfaces (butter paper, craft paper, ceramic plate, metal, paper, plastic) under UV 365 nm light (Fig. 14(c–h)). The designs exhibit distinct and sharply defined luminescence, which remains undetectable to the naked eye under normal lighting conditions. Furthermore, the durability of the printed designs is thoroughly assessed Fig. 14i shows the thermally treated AC patterns up to 200 °C. As can be seen from the figures, images exhibit good thermal stability and can retain their emission intensity up to 100 °C without significant degradation. Fig. 14j delves into the photo-stability of the encoded information using CDs(3 wt %)@CSO:3Eu3+ ink under UV 365 nm light exposure. When exposed to varying durations of UV irradiation (0–60 min), the AC patterns maintain visibility for up to 45 min before gradually diminishing in intensity. Fig. 14k depicts the ageing progression of AC patterns on a ceramic surface over a span of 60 days. Even after a 60 days period, the pattern remains discernible under 365 nm UV light, underscoring the enduring and consistent distribution as well as the intensity of the AC pattern on the metal substrate. Furthermore, another AC pattern developed on craft paper via brush mode is exposed to solvent treatment. After 30 min of immersion in an ethanol solution, the fluorescence intensity of the AC pattern gradually begins to diminish (Fig. 14l). These findings affirm the practical effectiveness of the developed ink for UV and thermally sensitive smart labels [128].

In summary, CSO:Eu3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs are effectively synthesized via the solid state method. The 3 mol % Eu3+ doped CSO NPs demonstrate the highest luminescence intensity. The successful integration of CDs into the CSO host matrix enable them to mitigate the adverse aggregated induced quenching effect in the solid state, positioning them as the predominant luminescent entities in CDs@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. Moreover, the detected energy transfer between Eu3+ ions and CDs substantially amplifies the PL enhancement in CDs(3wt%)@CSO:3Eu3+ NCs. Experimental findings affirm that the optimized NCs display exceptional thermal resilience. Utilizing a powder dusting technique LFPs and LLPs are visualized under 365 nm excitation unveiled Level I-III ridge features and Type I-VI groove patterns across diverse surfaces. The optimized NCs are employed in the creation of AC labels on various substrates. The resulting AC images showcase excellent fluorescence characteristics and precise binding specificity, offering enhanced contrast and heightened sensitivity. The comprehensive findings unequivocally illustrate the significant potential of the optimized NCs for deployment in solid-state devices, LFPs and LLPs detection and AC applications.

S. Priyanka Chakradhar: Methodology, Formal analysis. B.R. Radha Krushna: Investigation, Formal analysis. S.C. Sharma: Formal analysis. S.S. Mohapatra: Formal analysis. C. Krithika: Data curation. Augustine George: Validation. Sardar pasha: Investigation. K. Manjunatha: Software, Resources. Sheng Yun Wu: Software, Resources. Veera Vanitha: Visualization. H. Nagabhushana: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
